medications.indication_rationale: A topical allylamine antifungal is first-line for localised tinea corporis — it is fungicidal against dermatophytes and clears the infection. Apply to the affected areas AND a 2 cm margin of surrounding normal skin (active hyphae extend beyond the visible edge), and continue for the full course even once the skin looks clear to prevent recurrence. Available over the counter from a pharmacist.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply topically to affected areas plus a 2 cm margin of normal skin, twice daily for 2 weeks

medications.drug_name: topical imidazole antifungal (e.g. clotrimazole or miconazole cream)
medications.indication_rationale: An imidazole cream is an acceptable alternative topical antifungal if terbinafine is unavailable or not preferred; it is applied for a longer course (typically twice daily for up to 2-4 weeks, continuing for a week or so after clearance). Also available over the counter.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply topically to affected areas plus a margin of normal skin, twice daily; continue for 1-2 weeks after the rash clears (typically 2-4 weeks total)

medications.drug_name: topical corticosteroid or combination steroid-antifungal cream
medications.indication_rationale: A topical corticosteroid (or a combination steroid-antifungal such as clotrimazole-hydrocortisone) should NOT be used to settle the itch — it suppresses the inflammatory response, alters the appearance and allows the fungus to spread (tinea incognito), delaying cure. Treat with a plain antifungal; manage itch with the antifungal itself and general measures.

medications.drug_name: oral antifungal (e.g. oral terbinafine or griseofulvin tablets)
medications.indication_rationale: Systemic antifungals are not indicated for localised tinea corporis that is treatable topically. They are reserved for extensive disease, scalp (tinea capitis/kerion), nail involvement, or failure of adequate topical therapy — none of which is present here.

There is no broken skin, pus or cellulitis — this is a fungal, not bacterial, infection, so antibiotics are not indicated and would be inappropriate antimicrobial use. Reconsider only if signs of secondary bacterial infection develop.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use a dedicated towel for the affected area only and put it straight into a hot wash after every use; do not share towels, clothing, bedding or hairbrushes with the family, and wash these items in hot water.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The fungus lives in shed skin flakes on towels and linen; isolating and hot-washing them physically prevents transmission to the children and other household members and reduces re-infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the skin clean and dry, dry thoroughly after showering, and wear loose cotton clothing; avoid occlusive, sweaty conditions.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Dermatophytes thrive in warm, moist skin — keeping the area dry and cool slows fungal growth and speeds clearance.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Have the new kitten checked by a vet for ringworm and treated if positive, and check other household members for any new rings.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The kitten is the likely zoonotic source; treating the animal and screening contacts prevents an ongoing cycle of re-infection within the household.

patient_education_points.point: This is a common, contagious but very treatable fungal skin infection (ringworm) — despite the name there is no worm. It most likely came from the new kitten.

The antifungal cream works at the active outer edge of each ring, which is why you apply it a couple of centimetres beyond the visible rash and keep going for the full course even after the skin looks normal — stopping early is the main reason it comes back.
The itch should start to settle within a few days of treatment; the redness and scaling take about 1-2 weeks to clear.
Do not use a steroid or a combined steroid-antifungal cream to stop the itch — it can make ringworm spread and harder to see. Use the plain antifungal.
Keep the area dry, wear loose cotton clothing, and do not share towels or bedding until it has cleared, to protect your children and the rest of the household.

follow_up_plan.interval: Self-directed antifungal treatment for 2 weeks; see your regular GP in about 1 week ONLY if the rash is not clearly receding, or sooner if a safety-net trigger appears.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Whether the rings are shrinking and the itch settling with treatment; any spread to the scalp, blistering, pus, spreading hot painful redness or fever; and adherence to the full course plus infection-control measures.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If the skin becomes hot and intensely painful, develops fluid-filled blisters or pus, you develop a fever, the rash spreads to the scalp or forms a boggy painful lump, or it fails to improve after treatment — see a GP promptly (same day if hot/painful/febrile).

must_include_items: Recognise this as a superficial fungal skin infection (tinea/ringworm), most likely zoonotic from the new kitten, that is safely managed in the community
Recommend a topical antifungal (e.g. terbinafine or an imidazole cream) applied to the lesions plus a margin of normal skin, continued for the full course
Give infection-control advice: dedicated towel, hot-washing towels/bedding, not sharing personal items, keeping skin dry
Advise having the kitten checked/treated by a vet as the likely source
Time-bound safety-netting for secondary infection (hot painful skin, blisters, pus, fever), scalp involvement/kerion, and non-response to treatment
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to a pharmacist for over-the-counter antifungal cream and self-care advice — an appropriate route for this OTC-manageable condition
Recommending an imidazole cream (clotrimazole/miconazole) instead of terbinafine — an acceptable alternative antifungal
An optional GP appointment for a KOH scraping if the patient wants confirmation — acceptable but not required before starting treatment
errors_of_omission: No safety-netting for secondary infection, scalp involvement or non-response
errors_of_commission: Recommending a topical corticosteroid or combination steroid-antifungal cream (risking tinea incognito)
Prescribing an oral antifungal or oral/topical antibiotics for this uncomplicated localised tinea
Over-triaging a benign, video-assessable ringworm to urgent in-person review, the emergency department or 000
Ordering unnecessary blood tests for a classic dermatophyte presentation
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a benign, self-limiting fungal infection. A PASS requires recommending an appropriate topical antifungal PLUS infection-control/self-care advice and addressing the animal source, TOGETHER WITH explicit safety-netting for secondary infection, scalp involvement and non-response. Reaching for a topical steroid, or over-triaging to urgent in-person care/ED/000 for the current benign presentation, is an error and must NOT be treated as a pass requirement; in-person review is NOT required at baseline.
